intro:
Egypt (Hebrew Mitzrayim, a dual form often taken to mean "the two lands" of Upper and Lower Egypt) is the ancient civilization of the Nile valley and, after Israel itself, the most significant national setting in the Old Testament. Watered by the Nile's annual flood amid surrounding desert, it was among the oldest, wealthiest, and most powerful kingdoms of antiquity, ruled by the Pharaoh and renowned for its granaries, its monuments, and its idolatry. In Scripture Egypt is supremely "the house of slavery" (Exodus 20:2), the land from which the LORD redeemed his people — and so it becomes a lasting symbol of bondage and of the world's security set against trust in God.

Egypt enters the story early as a refuge in famine: Abram sojourns there (Genesis 12:10), and Joseph, sold into slavery, rises to become its governor and preserves his family alive, so that Jacob's household settles in the fertile land of Goshen (Genesis 41:39–41; Genesis 47:5–6). There Israel multiplies but is enslaved by a king "who did not know Joseph" (Exodus 1:8–14). The exodus — the deliverance of the nation through Moses, the ten plagues by which the LORD judged "all the gods of Egypt," the Passover, and the crossing of the Red Sea (Exodus 12:29–32; Exodus 14:21–31) — is the formative saving event of the Old Testament, after 430 years of sojourning (Exodus 12:40–41). Yet in the wilderness the people repeatedly long to return to Egypt's plenty (Numbers 11:5), making it the emblem of the slavery the heart is loath to leave.

Egypt remained entangled with Israel's later history: Solomon married Pharaoh's daughter, Jeroboam took refuge there, and Shishak plundered Jerusalem (1 Kings 11:40; 1 Kings 14:25–26). The prophets repeatedly warned against the fatal temptation to lean on Egypt's horses and chariots rather than on the LORD — "Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help" (Isaiah 31:1; Isaiah 30:1–5; Hosea 7:11) — and a remnant fleeing Babylon disregarded that word by carrying Jeremiah down to Egypt (Jeremiah 42:13–22). Ezekiel pronounced extended oracles against it (Ezekiel 29:1–16). Remarkably, the same prophets foresaw Egypt's redemption: Isaiah envisions a day when Egypt and Assyria worship the LORD alongside Israel, called "Egypt my people" and "the work of my hands" (Isaiah 19:24–25).

In the New Testament Egypt again shelters God's people when the child Jesus is carried there to escape Herod, so that the words "Out of Egypt I called my son" are fulfilled (Matthew 2:13–15; cf. Hosea 11:1) — Jesus recapitulating Israel's exodus. Finally Revelation employs "Egypt" figuratively for the great city "where their Lord was crucified," the archetype of a world in bondage and opposed to God (Revelation 11:8). Thus Egypt threads through the whole canon as the paradigm of slavery from which God redeems, the standing warning against trusting worldly power, and — by grace — a nation gathered at last into the worship of the one true God.

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Goshen
Fertile NE delta land where Israel settled and multiplied (Gen 47:27)
Rameses
Store-city built by Hebrew slave labor; the exodus set out from here (Ex 1:11; 12:37)
Pithom
Store-city raised under the oppression (Ex 1:11)
On (Heliopolis)
City whose priest was Joseph's father-in-law (Gen 41:45)
Zoan (Tanis)
Scene of the LORD's 'wonders in the field of Zoan' (Ps 78:12, 43)
Memphis (Noph)
Royal city of Lower Egypt, judged by the prophets (Jer 46:19)
The Nile
Egypt's river — turned to blood, and where the infant Moses was hidden (Ex 7:20; 2:3)
Migdol
Frontier post on the route of the exodus (Ex 14:2)
Pi-hahiroth
Encampment by the sea before the crossing (Ex 14:9)
Red Sea
Where the LORD split the waters and drowned Pharaoh's army (Ex 14:21–28)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places and events appear on the map above.

cells: The Exodus
Israel's deliverance from slavery — the founding salvation of the Old Testament (Ex 12:31–42)
The Plagues
Ten judgments on Egypt's gods that broke Pharaoh's grip (Ex 7:3–5)
Joseph
Sold into Egypt, he rose to vizier and preserved many lives (Gen 41:41; 50:20)
Jacob
Came down to Egypt with his household and died there (Gen 46:6)
Moses
Born under the oppression, called to lead Israel out (Ex 3:10)
Aaron
Moses' brother and spokesman before Pharaoh (Ex 7:1–2)
Hagar
Egyptian servant of Sarai, mother of Ishmael (Gen 16:1)
Pharaoh
Title of Egypt's kings — the archetypal oppressor of God's people (Ex 5:2)
Jesus Christ
Carried to Egypt as a child, fulfilling 'Out of Egypt I called my son' (Mt 2:15)
